title: Decode Ways
description: Given digits string, count decodings (1->A ... 26->Z). Example: 226 -> 3

examples.explanation:
decode(0): s[0]='2'≠'0' → count=decode(1) + (check 22: 10≤22≤26? yes → +decode(2))
  decode(1): s[1]='2'≠'0' → count=decode(2) + (check 26: 10≤26≤26? yes → +decode(3))
    decode(2): s[2]='6'≠'0' → count=decode(3) + (check beyond end: no two-digit)
      decode(3): i=3==len(s) → return 1
    decode(2): count=1+0=1 → return 1
    decode(3): i=3==len(s) → return 1
  decode(1): count=1+1=2 → return 2
  decode(2): s[2]='6'≠'0' → count=decode(3) + (no two-digit) = 1+0=1 → return 1
decode(0): count=2+1=3 → return 3

decode(0): s[0]='1'≠'0' → count=decode(1) + (check 12: 10≤12≤26? yes → +decode(2))
  decode(1): s[1]='2'≠'0' → count=decode(2) + (no two-digit beyond end)
    decode(2): i=2==len(s) → return 1
  decode(1): count=1+0=1 → return 1
  decode(2): i=2==len(s) → return 1
decode(0): count=1+1=2 → return 2

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Recursion O(2^n)
approaches.summary: Recursively try all possible decodings by considering each digit as a single character (if not '0') and each pair of digits as a character (if between 10 and 26). This explores all combinations but is inefficient due to repeated subproblems.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursion for Decode Ways
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution exploring all possible decodings of a digit string.
ladders.explanation:
The intuition behind this brute force approach is to systematically explore every possible way to decode the digit string by making decisions at each position. At any given index in the string, we have two choices: either decode the current single digit (if it's not '0', since '0' alone doesn't map to any letter) OR decode the current two-digit number (if it's between 10 and 26 inclusive). This creates a binary decision tree where each path represents a valid decoding sequence. The algorithm works by recursively branching at each position: if we can decode a single digit, we move forward one position; if we can decode two digits, we move forward two positions. When we reach the end of the string (base case), we've found one valid decoding. The time complexity is O(2^n) because in the worst case (like '1111...'), each position creates two recursive branches, leading to exponential growth. Space complexity is O(n) for the recursion stack depth. This connects directly to the problem statement by implementing the exact counting logic described. Common mistakes include forgetting to handle '0' properly (it can only be part of '10' or '20'), not checking bounds when accessing two-digit substrings, and missing base cases. The real pattern involves careful index management and condition checking.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Define recursive function decode(i) that returns number of ways to decode substring starting at index i
2. Base case: if i == len(s), return 1 (successfully decoded entire string)
3. If s[i] == '0', return 0 (invalid decoding)
4. Initialize count = decode(i+1) (try single digit decoding)
5. If i+1 < len(s) and 10 <= int(s[i:i+2]) <= 26:
   count += decode(i+2) (try two-digit decoding)
6. Return count

Time: O(2^n) - exponential due to repeated subproblems
Space: O(n) - recursion stack depth

examples.trace:
decode(0): s[0]='2'≠'0' → count=decode(1) + (check 22: 10≤22≤26? yes → +decode(2))
  decode(1): s[1]='2'≠'0' → count=decode(2) + (check 26: 10≤26≤26? yes → +decode(3))
    decode(2): s[2]='6'≠'0' → count=decode(3) + (check beyond end: no two-digit)
      decode(3): i=3==len(s) → return 1
    decode(2): count=1+0=1 → return 1
    decode(3): i=3==len(s) → return 1
  decode(1): count=1+1=2 → return 2
  decode(2): s[2]='6'≠'0' → count=decode(3) + (no two-digit) = 1+0=1 → return 1
decode(0): count=2+1=3 → return 3

decode(0): s[0]='1'≠'0' → count=decode(1) + (check 12: 10≤12≤26? yes → +decode(2))
  decode(1): s[1]='2'≠'0' → count=decode(2) + (no two-digit beyond end)
    decode(2): i=2==len(s) → return 1
  decode(1): count=1+0=1 → return 1
  decode(2): i=2==len(s) → return 1
decode(0): count=1+1=2 → return 2

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Decoding Function
ladders.desc: The core recursive function that counts decodings from a given index.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine represents the heart of the brute force approach—a recursive function that, given a starting index in the digit string, returns the number of valid decodings from that position onward. The intuition is that decoding is inherently recursive: to count all decodings from position i, we need to count decodings from i+1 (if we take one digit) plus decodings from i+2 (if we take two valid digits). This mirrors the problem's structure perfectly. The function must handle three key aspects: base cases (when we reach the end), invalid cases (when we encounter '0' alone), and valid branching decisions. It connects to the full problem by being the main recursive engine—the full solution essentially calls this function with index 0. Common mistakes include incorrect base case handling (returning 1 only when reaching exact end), forgetting that '0' cannot be decoded alone, and not checking array bounds before accessing two-digit substrings. The time complexity for this sub-routine on a substring of length m is O(2^m), and space is O(m) for recursion depth.

Algorithm for decode(i):
1. If i == len(s): return 1 (successfully decoded everything)
2. If s[i] == '0': return 0 (invalid path)
3. ways = decode(i+1) (take single digit)
4. If i+1 < len(s) and 10 ≤ int(s[i:i+2]) ≤ 26:
   ways += decode(i+2) (take two digits)
5. Return ways

This is a classic recursive backtracking pattern with pruning.

examples.trace:
decode(1): i=1≠len(s), s[1]='2'≠'0' → ways=decode(2) + (check s[1:3]='26': 10≤26≤26? yes → +decode(3))
  decode(2): i=2≠len(s), s[2]='6'≠'0' → ways=decode(3) + (no two-digit: i+1=3≥len(s))
    decode(3): i=3==len(s) → return 1
  decode(2): ways=1+0=1 → return 1
  decode(3): i=3==len(s) → return 1
decode(1): ways=1+1=2 → return 2

decode(0): i=0≠len(s), s[0]='1'≠'0' → ways=decode(1) + (check s[0:2]='10': 10≤10≤26? yes → +decode(2))
  decode(1): i=1≠len(s), s[1]='0'=='0' → return 0 (invalid)
  decode(2): i=2≠len(s), s[2]='1'≠'0' → ways=decode(3) + (no two-digit)
    decode(3): i=3==len(s) → return 1
  decode(2): ways=1+0=1 → return 1
decode(0): ways=0+1=1 → return 1

ladders.title: Core Logic — Branching Decision at Each Position
ladders.desc: The decision logic: take one digit or two digits based on validity conditions.
ladders.explanation:
This core logic represents the fundamental decision-making process at each step of the decoding. The intuition is that decoding is a sequential process where at each position, we must decide whether to consume one digit or two digits, based on specific validity rules. For a single digit: it must not be '0' (since 0 doesn't map to A-Z). For two digits: they must form a number between 10 and 26 inclusive (covering J through Z, plus 10=J, 20=T). This creates a binary choice tree. The logic connects to the recursive function by being the conditional check that determines whether to make recursive calls. It's essential because it encodes the mapping rules from the problem statement. Common mistakes include: treating '0' as valid single digit, allowing numbers like '07' as valid two-digit decoding (should be invalid since 7 is not 07), and not checking array bounds before substring access. This logic has O(1) time per decision and O(1) space.

Pseudocode for decision at index i:
if s[i] != '0':
    # Option 1: take single digit
    ways += recursive_call(i+1)
    
if i+1 < len(s):
    two_digit = int(s[i:i+2])
    if 10 <= two_digit <= 26:
        # Option 2: take two digits
        ways += recursive_call(i+2)

This represents the branching condition that generates the exponential search space.

examples.trace:
At i=0: s[0]='2'≠'0' → valid single digit → branch to i+1=1
Check two-digit: i+1=1<len(s), s[0:2]='22', int=22, 10≤22≤26 → valid → branch to i+2=2
Total: 2 branches created

At i=0: s[0]='0'=='0' → invalid single digit → no branch to i+1
Check two-digit: i+1=1<len(s), s[0:2]='06', int=6, 6<10 → invalid → no branch to i+2
Total: 0 branches created

ladders.title: Building Block — Validating Two-Digit Encoding
ladders.desc: Checking if two consecutive digits form a valid letter encoding (10-26).
ladders.explanation:
This building block focuses specifically on validating whether two consecutive digits can be decoded as a single letter. The intuition comes from the problem's mapping: numbers 1-26 map to A-Z, but for two-digit numbers, we must consider the range 10-26 inclusive (since 1-9 are single-digit mappings). This validation requires: 1) ensuring we have two digits available (index bounds check), 2) converting the two-character substring to an integer, and 3) checking if the integer falls in [10, 26]. It connects to the core logic as the condition for the 'take two digits' branch. This is a critical sub-problem because getting this validation wrong leads to incorrect counts (e.g., allowing '27' which maps to nothing, or missing '20' which is valid). Common mistakes include: forgetting to check index bounds (leading to IndexError), not converting to integer (string comparison won't work), and incorrect range boundaries (should be inclusive). Time and space are O(1).

Algorithm:
1. If i+1 >= len(s): return False (not enough digits)
2. two_digit_str = s[i:i+2]
3. two_digit_int = int(two_digit_str)
4. Return True if 10 ≤ two_digit_int ≤ 26, else False

Note: We don't need to check for leading '0' explicitly because int('07')=7 which is <10, so invalid anyway.

examples.trace:
i=0, i+1=1<len(s)=3 → enough digits
two_digit_str = s[0:2] = '22'
int('22') = 22
Check: 10 ≤ 22 ≤ 26? yes → return true

i=0, i+1=1<len(s)=2 → enough digits
two_digit_str = s[0:2] = '27'
int('27') = 27
Check: 10 ≤ 27 ≤ 26? no → return false

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Single Digit Validity Check
ladders.desc: Checking if a single digit can be decoded (not '0').
ladders.explanation:
This basic operation is the simplest validity check in the decoding process: determining whether a single digit can be decoded as a letter. The intuition is straightforward: digits 1-9 map to A-I, while digit 0 does not map to any letter (there's no '0' in the mapping). Therefore, the only invalid single digit is '0'. This operation connects to the building block as the first check in the decision logic—before even considering two-digit options, we must ensure the current digit isn't '0'. It's fundamental because if we encounter '0' at any position (except as part of '10' or '20'), the entire decoding path becomes invalid. Common mistakes include: treating '0' as valid, or incorrectly checking for other invalid digits (all digits 1-9 are valid). This operation is O(1) time and space.

Algorithm:
1. Let digit_char = s[i]
2. Return True if digit_char != '0', else False

That's it! This simple check is crucial for correct algorithm behavior.

examples.trace:
digit_char = s[0] = '2'
'2' != '0' → return true

digit_char = s[1] = '0'
'0' == '0' → return false

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Indexing and Substrings
ladders.desc: Accessing characters and substrings in a string by index.
ladders.explanation:
This foundational concept involves basic string manipulation: accessing individual characters and extracting substrings using indices. The intuition is that strings are sequences of characters, and we can refer to specific positions using zero-based indexing. For the decoding problem, we need to: 1) check individual digits (e.g., s[i] to see if it's '0'), and 2) check pairs of digits (e.g., s[i:i+2] to get a two-digit number). This connects to the basic operation as the mechanism to get the digit character for validation. It's the absolute prerequisite—without understanding how to access string elements, none of the higher-level logic can be implemented. Common beginner mistakes include: using 1-based indexing (Python uses 0-based), misunderstanding slice notation (s[i:j] goes from i inclusive to j exclusive), and not checking bounds before accessing. Time complexity for accessing a character or substring is O(1) for practical purposes, space depends on substring length.

Key operations:
1. s[i] - get character at index i (must have 0 ≤ i < len(s))
2. s[i:j] - get substring from i (inclusive) to j (exclusive)
3. len(s) - get string length

Example: s='hello', s[0]='h', s[1:4]='ell', len(s)=5

examples.trace:
String '226' has indices: 0:'2', 1:'2', 2:'6'
Access s[1]: index 1 → character '2'
Access s[1:3]: slice from index 1 (inclusive) to 3 (exclusive) → characters at indices 1 and 2 → '26'

String '12' has indices: 0:'1', 1:'2'
Access s[0]: index 0 → character '1'
Access s[0:2]: slice from 0 to 2 → indices 0 and 1 → '12'

approaches.name: Approach 2: Top-Down DP with Memoization O(n)
approaches.summary: Use recursion with memoization to store results of subproblems (e.g., number of ways to decode from a given index), avoiding repeated work. This reduces time complexity to linear by caching intermediate results.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) for memoization and recursion stack

ladders.title: Full Problem — Count Decodings with Top-Down DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution using recursion with memoization to count all possible decodings of a digit string.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind this approach is to break the problem into smaller subproblems: at each position in the string, we can either decode the current single digit (if it's not '0') or decode the current two-digit combination (if it's between 10 and 26). This creates a recursive tree where we explore both possibilities. However, naive recursion would have exponential time complexity due to repeated subproblems. Memoization solves this by storing the results of subproblems (number of ways to decode from a given index) in a cache, ensuring each subproblem is solved only once. The algorithm starts from index 0 and recursively explores valid decodings: if the current digit is '0', there are 0 ways (invalid); otherwise, count ways from next index. Additionally, if the next two digits form a valid number between 10 and 26, add ways from index+2. Base cases: if index reaches the end, return 1 (successful decoding); if index is beyond end, return 0. Time complexity is O(n) because each of n indices is computed once and cached. Space complexity is O(n) for the memoization cache and recursion stack depth. This connects to the parent problem by providing the complete counting mechanism. Common mistakes include not handling '0' correctly (it cannot be decoded alone) and forgetting to check bounds for two-digit decoding. Real code pattern: define a recursive function dfs(i) that returns number of ways from index i, with memoization using a dictionary or array.

examples.trace: Start dfs(0): s='226', memo={}. Check index 0: not '0', so ways = dfs(1). dfs(1): not '0', ways = dfs(2). dfs(2): not '0', ways = dfs(3). dfs(3): index=3 equals len(s)=3, return 1 (base case). Back to dfs(2): ways=1, check two-digit: s[2:2+2]='6' (out of bounds, index 4), skip. memo[2]=1. Back to dfs(1): ways=1, check two-digit: s[1:3]='26', valid (10-26), add dfs(3)=1, total ways=2. memo[1]=2. Back to dfs(0): ways=2, check two-digit: s[0:2]='22', valid, add dfs(2)=1, total ways=3. memo[0]=3. Return 3.
Start dfs(0): s='06', memo={}. Check index 0: digit is '0', return 0 immediately (invalid). No further recursion. Return 0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive DFS with Memoization
ladders.desc: Implement the recursive function that explores decodings from a given index and caches results.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is to define a function dfs(i) that returns the number of ways to decode the substring starting at index i. This function encapsulates the decision logic: if the current digit is '0', return 0 (no valid decoding); otherwise, initialize count with dfs(i+1) for single-digit decoding. Then, if i+1 < len(s) and the two-digit number s[i:i+2] is between 10 and 26, add dfs(i+2). Base cases: if i == len(s), return 1 (empty string decoded successfully); if i > len(s), return 0 (invalid). Memoization is achieved by storing results in a dictionary or array before returning, so repeated calls with same i return cached value. This reduces time from exponential to linear. Time complexity for this sub-routine is O(n) per call with memoization, but overall O(n) across all calls. Space complexity is O(n) for cache. This feeds into the full problem by being the core recursive engine. Common mistakes include incorrect base case handling or not checking bounds for two-digit decoding. Real code pattern: def dfs(i): if i in memo: return memo[i]; if i == n: return 1; if s[i] == '0': return 0; ways = dfs(i+1); if i+1 < n and 10 <= int(s[i:i+2]) <= 26: ways += dfs(i+2); memo[i] = ways; return ways.

examples.trace: s='12', n=2, memo={}. dfs(0): i=0, not in memo. i=0 != n=2. s[0]='1' != '0'. ways = dfs(1). dfs(1): i=1, not in memo. i=1 != n=2. s[1]='2' != '0'. ways = dfs(2). dfs(2): i=2 == n=2, return 1. Back to dfs(1): ways=1, check two-digit: i+1=2 not < n=2, skip. memo[1]=1, return 1. Back to dfs(0): ways=1, check two-digit: i+1=1 < n=2, s[0:2]='12', int=12 valid (10-26), add dfs(2)=1, total ways=2. memo[0]=2, return 2.
s='0', n=1, memo={}. dfs(0): i=0, not in memo. i=0 != n=1. s[0]='0', return 0 immediately. No memo update for i=0 as it's a direct return.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Decision Points for Single vs. Two-Digit Decoding
ladders.desc: Determine when to decode a single digit or a two-digit combination at a given index.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that at each position i in the string, we have two potential decoding choices, but only if they are valid according to the mapping rules. First, check if the current digit s[i] is '0': if yes, it cannot be decoded alone (since 0 does not map to a letter), so this path is invalid. Otherwise, we can always decode s[i] as a single digit (1-9 maps to A-I), which corresponds to moving to index i+1. Second, check if we can decode two digits starting at i: this requires that i+1 is within bounds (i+1 < len(s)) and that the two-digit number formed by s[i] and s[i+1] is between 10 and 26 inclusive. This is because 10-26 map to J-Z. If valid, this corresponds to moving to index i+2. The core logic combines these decisions: ways(i) = (if s[i] != '0' then ways(i+1) else 0) + (if two-digit valid then ways(i+2) else 0). This recursive relation is the heart of the DP solution. Time complexity for evaluating this logic at one index is O(1), but overall it drives the O(n) algorithm. Space complexity is minimal for the logic itself. This feeds into the sub-routine by defining the recurrence. Common mistakes include not checking for '0' or incorrectly validating two-digit numbers (e.g., including 00 or numbers >26). Real code pattern: if s[i] == '0': return 0; ways = dfs(i+1); if i+1 < n and 10 <= int(s[i:i+2]) <= 26: ways += dfs(i+2).

examples.trace: At i=0, s='12'. s[0]='1' != '0', so single-digit valid: add dfs(1). Check two-digit: i+1=1 < n=2, s[0:2]='12', int=12, valid (10-26), so add dfs(2). Total ways = dfs(1) + dfs(2).
At i=0, s='30'. s[0]='3' != '0', so single-digit valid: add dfs(1). Check two-digit: i+1=1 < n=2, s[0:2]='30', int=30, invalid (>26), so no addition. Total ways = dfs(1).

ladders.title: Building Block — Memoization for Overlapping Subproblems
ladders.desc: Implement caching to store and reuse results of subproblems in recursion.
ladders.explanation: The intuition behind memoization is that in the recursive decoding process, the same subproblem (e.g., number of ways to decode from index i) may be computed multiple times due to the branching nature of decisions. For example, in string '111', dfs(2) is called from both dfs(0) and dfs(1). Without caching, this leads to exponential time complexity. Memoization solves this by storing the result of dfs(i) in a data structure (like a dictionary or array) the first time it is computed. On subsequent calls with the same i, we simply return the cached value instead of recomputing. This transforms the time complexity to linear, as each index is computed at most once. The algorithm: initialize a memo cache (e.g., dict or list of size n+1 with -1 for uncomputed). In dfs(i), first check if memo[i] is already computed; if yes, return it. Otherwise, compute the result using the recurrence, store it in memo[i], and then return it. Time complexity for this building block is O(1) per cache access, but it enables overall O(n). Space complexity is O(n) for the cache. This feeds into the core logic by making the recursion efficient. Common mistakes include not initializing cache properly or forgetting to check cache before computation. Real code pattern: memo = {}; def dfs(i): if i in memo: return memo[i]; # compute result; memo[i] = result; return result.

examples.trace: Compute dfs(0): not in memo. Calls dfs(1). dfs(1): not in memo. Calls dfs(2). dfs(2): not in memo. Calls dfs(3). dfs(3): base case return 1. Back to dfs(2): ways=1, check two-digit: i+1=3 out of bounds, skip. memo[2]=1, return 1. Back to dfs(1): ways=1, check two-digit: s[1:3]='11', valid, add dfs(3)=1, total ways=2. memo[1]=2, return 2. Back to dfs(0): ways=2, check two-digit: s[0:2]='11', valid, add dfs(2)=1, total ways=3. memo[0]=3, return 3. Final memo: {2:1, 1:2, 0:3}.

Compute dfs(0): not in memo. Calls dfs(1). dfs(1): not in memo. Calls dfs(2). dfs(2): base case return 1, memo[2]=1. Back to dfs(1): ways=1, check two-digit: i+1=2 out of bounds, skip. memo[1]=1, return 1. Back to dfs(0): ways=1, check two-digit: s[0:2]='12', valid, add dfs(2)=1, total ways=2. memo[0]=2, return 2. Final memo: {2:1, 1:1, 0:2}.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Check Validity of Two-Digit Decoding
ladders.desc: Determine if a two-digit substring represents a valid number between 10 and 26.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that in the decoding mapping, only numbers from 10 to 26 correspond to letters (J-Z), so when considering decoding two digits starting at index i, we must verify that the substring s[i:i+2] forms an integer in this range. This operation involves three steps: first, ensure that i+1 is within the string bounds (i+1 < len(s)) to avoid index errors. Second, extract the two-character substring s[i] and s[i+1]. Third, convert this substring to an integer and check if it is between 10 and 26 inclusive. If all conditions hold, the two-digit decoding is valid; otherwise, it is invalid. For example, '09' is invalid because 9 is less than 10, and '30' is invalid because 30 is greater than 26. This operation is a fundamental building block because it directly influences the decision logic in the DP recurrence. Time complexity is O(1) as it involves constant-time string slicing and integer conversion. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into the core logic by providing the validity check for the two-digit option. Common mistakes include not checking bounds first, or incorrectly handling numbers like '00' (which is 0, invalid) or '01' (which is 1, invalid). Real code pattern: if i+1 < n and 10 <= int(s[i:i+2]) <= 26: # valid.

examples.trace: Check bounds: i+1=1, n=2, so 1 < 2, within bounds. Substring s[0:2]='12'. Convert to int: 12. Check range: 10 <= 12 <= 26, true. Therefore, valid.
Check bounds: i+1=1, n=2, so 1 < 2, within bounds. Substring s[0:2]='30'. Convert to int: 30. Check range: 10 <= 30 <= 26, false (30 > 26). Therefore, invalid.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — String Indexing and Slicing
ladders.desc: Access characters and substrings in a string using indices.
ladders.explanation: The intuition is that a string in programming is a sequence of characters, and we can access individual characters or ranges of characters using indices. Indexing refers to getting a single character at a specific position: for a string s, s[i] returns the character at index i, where indices start at 0 for the first character and go up to len(s)-1 for the last. Slicing refers to getting a substring from a start index to an end index: s[i:j] returns characters from index i up to but not including j. If j is beyond the length, it safely returns up to the end. For example, if s = 'hello', s[0] is 'h', s[1:4] is 'ell'. This operation is fundamental because in the decoding problem, we need to check individual digits (e.g., s[i] for '0') and two-digit combinations (e.g., s[i:i+2]). Understanding bounds is crucial: accessing an index out of range (e.g., s[5] for 'hello') causes an error, but slicing with out-of-range end (e.g., s[3:10]) returns 'lo' without error. Time complexity for indexing and slicing is O(1) for small strings, but slicing may involve copying. Space complexity is O(k) for a slice of length k. This feeds into the basic operation by enabling extraction of digits. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in slicing or forgetting that indices start at 0. Real code pattern: char = s[i]; substring = s[i:j].

examples.trace: String s has length 3, indices 0,1,2. s[0] accesses character at index 0: '2'. s[0:2] slices from index 0 to 2 (exclusive), so includes indices 0 and 1: characters '2' and '2', resulting substring '22'.
String s has length 1, indices 0 only. s[0] accesses character at index 0: '1'. s[0:3] slices from index 0 to 3, but since length is 1, it returns characters from 0 up to end: '1'.

approaches.name: Approach 3: Bottom-Up DP O(n)
approaches.summary: Iteratively build a DP array where dp[i] represents the number of ways to decode the substring up to index i. Use recurrence based on single and double-digit decodings, starting from the base cases.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Bottom-Up DP Solution for Decode Ways
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the bottom-up DP approach to count all possible decodings of a digit string.
ladders.explanation: The bottom-up DP approach solves the decode ways problem by building a DP array iteratively from left to right. The key insight is that the number of ways to decode a string up to position i depends on two possibilities: treating the last digit as a single character (if it's not '0') and treating the last two digits as a double-digit character (if they form a valid number between 10 and 26). We define dp[i] as the number of ways to decode the substring s[0:i] (first i characters). The recurrence relation is: dp[i] = (dp[i-1] if s[i-1] != '0') + (dp[i-2] if the last two digits form a valid number between 10 and 26). Base cases: dp[0] = 1 (empty string has 1 way), and dp[1] depends on whether the first character is '0'. The algorithm iterates from i=2 to n, applying the recurrence. Time complexity is O(n) since we process each character once, and space complexity is O(n) for the DP array. This approach avoids recursion overhead and builds the solution systematically. Common mistakes include mishandling '0' characters (which cannot be decoded alone) and incorrect base cases. The connection to L1 is that this level implements the complete DP recurrence and base case handling.

examples.trace: Initialize dp array of size 4 (n=3). dp[0]=1 (empty string). For i=1: s[0]='2' != '0', so dp[1]=dp[0]=1. For i=2: s[1]='2' != '0', so add dp[1]=1. Last two digits '22' form 22 (valid 10-26), so add dp[0]=1. dp[2]=1+1=2. For i=3: s[2]='6' != '0', so add dp[2]=2. Last two digits '26' form 26 (valid), so add dp[1]=1. dp[3]=2+1=3. Return dp[3]=3.

Initialize dp array of size 3 (n=2). dp[0]=1. For i=1: s[0]='0' == '0', so no single-digit addition. dp[1]=0. For i=2: s[1]='6' != '0', so add dp[1]=0. Last two digits '06' form 6 (invalid, <10), so no double-digit addition. dp[2]=0. Return dp[2]=0.

ladders.title: Key Sub-Routine — DP Recurrence Application
ladders.desc: Implement the core recurrence relation that computes dp[i] from previous values.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on applying the recurrence relation at each step i (where i >= 2). Given the DP array dp, the string s, and current index i, we compute dp[i] as: first, if s[i-1] is not '0', add dp[i-1] (treat last digit as single character). Second, if the substring s[i-2:i] (last two digits) forms a number between 10 and 26 inclusive, add dp[i-2] (treat as double-digit character). The intuition is that each decoding way up to i can be formed by extending decodings up to i-1 with a single character or up to i-2 with a double character. The algorithm checks these conditions and sums contributions. Time per step is O(1), and overall for n steps it's O(n). Space is O(1) extra per step. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors in indexing and incorrect validation of double-digit numbers (e.g., missing that '01' is invalid). This feeds into L0 by being the iterative step in the full solution. Real code pattern: if s[i-1] != '0': dp[i] += dp[i-1]; if 10 <= int(s[i-2:i]) <= 26: dp[i] += dp[i-2].

examples.trace: At i=2: s[1]='2' != '0', so add dp[1]=1. Last two digits '12' form 12 (valid 10-26), so add dp[0]=1. dp[2]=1+1=2.
At i=2: s[1]='6' != '0', so add dp[1]=0. Last two digits '06' form 6 (invalid, <10), so no addition. dp[2]=0.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Validating Single and Double-Digit Decodings
ladders.desc: Determine when a digit or pair of digits can be decoded into a character.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the validation logic for decodings. For a single digit: it must not be '0' because '0' does not map to any letter (A=1, B=2, ..., Z=26). For a pair of digits: they must form a number between 10 and 26 inclusive. This is because mappings are only defined for 1 to 26. The intuition is that the encoding is based on the alphabet positions, so invalid numbers cannot be decoded. The algorithm involves checking if a character is '0' for single-digit validation, and for double-digit, converting the two-character substring to an integer and comparing to the range. Time per validation is O(1). Space is O(1). Common mistakes include allowing '0' as valid or missing that numbers like '01' are invalid (since leading zero makes it 1, but as a string it's not between 10 and 26). This feeds into L1 by providing the conditions for the recurrence. Real code pattern: single_valid = s[i] != '0'; double_valid = 10 <= int(s[i:i+2]) <= 26.

examples.trace: Check if digit is '0': '5' != '0', so valid.
Convert to integer: 26. Check if 10 <= 26 <= 26: yes, so valid.

ladders.title: Building Block — DP Array Initialization and Base Cases
ladders.desc: Set up the DP array and handle base cases for the first one or two characters.
ladders.explanation: This level involves initializing the DP array and setting base cases. We create an array dp of size n+1, where n is the length of the string. dp[0] is set to 1, representing the empty string having one way to decode (intuition: it's the starting point for building solutions). For dp[1], we check the first character: if it is '0', then dp[1] = 0 (no ways to decode a string starting with '0'); otherwise, dp[1] = 1 (the single digit can be decoded as one letter). The intuition is that these base cases anchor the recurrence for longer strings. Time complexity is O(1) for initialization. Space is O(n) for the array. Common mistakes include setting dp[0] to 0 or incorrectly handling dp[1] for '0'. This feeds into L0 by providing the starting values for the iterative process. Real code pattern: dp = [0]*(n+1); dp[0]=1; if n>0: dp[1]=1 if s[0]!='0' else 0.

examples.trace: Initialize dp size 3: [0,0,0]. Set dp[0]=1. First char '1' != '0', so dp[1]=1. dp is now [1,1,0].
Initialize dp size 2: [0,0]. Set dp[0]=1. First char '0' == '0', so dp[1]=0. dp is [1,0].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — String Indexing and Substring Extraction
ladders.desc: Access characters and extract substrings from the input string.
ladders.explanation: This level covers fundamental string operations: accessing a character at a given index and extracting a substring of two characters. In many programming languages, strings are indexed starting from 0. For a string s of length n, s[i] gives the character at position i (0 <= i < n). To get a substring of two characters starting at i, we use s[i:i+2], which includes characters at i and i+1 (if i+1 < n). The intuition is that we need these operations to check single digits and pairs of digits for validation. Time complexity is O(1) for indexing and O(k) for substring extraction where k is length, but here k=2 so O(1). Space is O(1) for indexing, O(2) for substring. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors or assuming 1-based indexing. This feeds into L2 by providing the data needed for validation. Real code pattern: char = s[index]; two_char = s[start:start+2].

examples.trace: String 'hello' has indices 0:'h', 1:'e', 2:'l', 3:'l', 4:'o'. Access s[1] returns 'e'.
Extract substring from index 1 to 3 (exclusive): s[1:3] gives characters at 1:'o' and 2:'r', resulting in 'or'.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Number to Letter Mapping
ladders.desc: Learn the basic mapping from numbers 1-26 to letters A-Z.
ladders.explanation: This foundational level introduces the encoding scheme: each letter from A to Z is mapped to a number from 1 to 26, respectively. Specifically, A=1, B=2, C=3, ..., Z=26. The intuition is that in the problem, a string of digits can be decoded by grouping digits into numbers that correspond to these mappings. For example, '1' decodes to 'A', '26' decodes to 'Z'. It's important to note that '0' does not map to any letter, and numbers outside 1-26 are invalid. This is a simple lookup concept with no algorithm; it's just knowledge. Time and space are not applicable. Common mistakes include thinking '0' maps to a letter or that numbers above 26 are valid. This feeds into L2 by defining what constitutes a valid decoding. Real code pattern: mapping = {1:'A', 2:'B', ..., 26:'Z'}.

examples.trace: According to the mapping, 1 corresponds to the first letter of the alphabet, which is A.
26 corresponds to the 26th letter, which is Z.

approaches.name: Approach 4: Space-Optimized DP O(n)
approaches.summary: Optimize bottom-up DP by using only two variables to track the previous two states, since dp[i] depends only on dp[i-1] and dp[i-2]. This reduces space complexity to constant while maintaining linear time.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Space-Optimized DP Solution for Decode Ways
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of the space-optimized dynamic programming approach to count all possible decodings of a digit string.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete solution using the space-optimized DP approach. The intuition is that we can count decodings by treating each digit as either a single-digit decode (1-9) or part of a two-digit decode (10-26). Since dp[i] (ways to decode up to index i) depends only on dp[i-1] and dp[i-2], we can use just two variables instead of an entire array. We iterate through the string, maintaining 'prev1' (ways up to previous position) and 'prev2' (ways up to two positions back). At each position i, we initialize current = 0. If the current digit is not '0', we can decode it as a single letter (A-I), so add prev1 to current. If the two-digit substring ending at i forms a valid number 10-26, we can decode it as a single letter (J-Z), so add prev2 to current. Then update prev2 = prev1 and prev1 = current for the next iteration. The final answer is prev1. Time complexity is O(n) as we iterate once through the string. Space complexity is O(1) since we use only constant extra variables. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete counting logic with space optimization. Common mistakes include not handling '0' properly (which cannot be decoded alone), forgetting to check two-digit validity before adding prev2, and incorrect initialization for empty string or string starting with '0'. Real code pattern: Initialize prev1 = 1 (ways for empty string), prev2 = 0. For i from 0 to n-1: current = 0; if s[i] != '0': current += prev1; if i > 0 and 10 <= int(s[i-1:i+1]) <= 26: current += prev2; prev2 = prev1; prev1 = current. Return prev1.

examples.trace:
Initialize: prev1 = 1 (ways for empty string), prev2 = 0
Index 0 (s[0]='2'): current = 0; digit '2' != '0' → add prev1=1 → current=1; no two-digit check (i=0) → current=1; update: prev2=1, prev1=1
Index 1 (s[1]='2'): current = 0; digit '2' != '0' → add prev1=1 → current=1; two-digit '22' (10-26) → add prev2=1 → current=2; update: prev2=1, prev1=2
Index 2 (s[2]='6'): current = 0; digit '6' != '0' → add prev1=2 → current=2; two-digit '26' (10-26) → add prev2=1 → current=3; update: prev2=2, prev1=3
Return prev1=3

Initialize: prev1 = 1, prev2 = 0
Index 0 (s[0]='0'): current = 0; digit '0' == '0' → no single-digit add; no two-digit check (i=0) → current=0; update: prev2=1, prev1=0
Index 1 (s[1]='6'): current = 0; digit '6' != '0' → add prev1=0 → current=0; two-digit '06' = 6 (not 10-26) → no add; update: prev2=0, prev1=0
Return prev1=0

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Counting Valid Decodings at Each Position
ladders.desc: Implement the core counting logic that determines how many ways to decode ending at a specific position.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine calculates the number of decodings possible ending at position i, given the counts for previous positions. The intuition is that at position i, we can either decode the single digit s[i] (if it's 1-9) or decode the two-digit substring s[i-1:i+1] (if it's 10-26). If we decode s[i] alone, it contributes all ways from position i-1. If we decode the two-digit substring, it contributes all ways from position i-2. So total ways at i = (valid single-digit? ways[i-1] : 0) + (valid two-digit? ways[i-2] : 0). We implement this as a function countWaysAtPosition(i, s, prev1, prev2) that returns the current count. Time complexity is O(1) per call. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L0 by being the repeated operation in the main loop. Common mistakes include not checking that s[i] != '0' for single-digit decode (since '0' alone is invalid), and not verifying the two-digit number is between 10 and 26 inclusive. Real code pattern: def countWaysAtPosition(i, s, prev1, prev2): current = 0; if s[i] != '0': current += prev1; if i > 0 and 10 <= int(s[i-1:i+1]) <= 26: current += prev2; return current.

examples.trace: s[2] = '6' ≠ '0' → add prev1=2 → current=2; two-digit substring s[1:3] = '26' → int('26')=26 is between 10 and 26 → add prev2=1 → current=3; return 3
s[1] = '6' ≠ '0' → add prev1=0 → current=0; two-digit substring s[0:2] = '06' → int('06')=6 is not between 10 and 26 → no add; return 0

ladders.title: Core Logic — State Transition in Space-Optimized DP
ladders.desc: Understand how to maintain only two variables to track DP states instead of an entire array.
ladders.explanation: The core insight is that in the DP recurrence dp[i] = (valid single? dp[i-1] : 0) + (valid two-digit? dp[i-2] : 0), each position i depends only on the two previous states. Therefore, instead of storing all dp[0..n] in an array, we can use two variables: prev1 (representing dp[i-1]) and prev2 (representing dp[i-2]). As we iterate through the string, we compute current for position i using prev1 and prev2, then update prev2 = prev1 and prev1 = current for the next iteration. This reduces space from O(n) to O(1). The intuition is similar to Fibonacci sequence computation where we only need the last two values. Time complexity remains O(n). This connects to L1 by showing how the counting logic fits into the optimized state management. Common mistakes include incorrect initialization (prev1 should be 1 for empty string, prev2=0), updating variables in wrong order, or not resetting current each iteration. Real code pattern: prev1, prev2 = 1, 0; for i in range(n): current = countWaysAtPosition(i, s, prev1, prev2); prev2, prev1 = prev1, current.

examples.trace:
Initialize: prev1=1, prev2=0
i=0: current = (s[0]='1'≠'0'? prev1=1:0) + (no two-digit) = 1; update: prev2=1, prev1=1
i=1: current = (s[1]='2'≠'0'? prev1=1:0) + (two-digit '12' valid? prev2=1:0) = 1+1=2; update: prev2=1, prev1=2
Final prev1=2

Initialize: prev1=1, prev2=0
i=0: current = (s[0]='0'≠'0'? no:0) + (no two-digit) = 0; update: prev2=1, prev1=0
Final prev1=0

ladders.title: Building Block — Validating Two-Digit Decoding Conditions
ladders.desc: Check if a two-digit substring forms a valid number for decoding (10-26).
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves determining whether two consecutive digits can be decoded as a single letter (J-Z). The condition is: the two-digit number must be between 10 and 26 inclusive. We need to extract the substring s[i-1:i+1], convert it to an integer, and check the range. The intuition is that only numbers 10 through 26 map to letters J through Z in the encoding scheme (1->A, 2->B, ..., 26->Z). Numbers like 01, 27, 30 are invalid as two-digit decodes. Implementation: if i > 0 (to ensure we have two digits) and 10 <= int(s[i-1] + s[i]) <= 26. Time complexity is O(1) per check. Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L2 by being part of the state transition condition. Common mistakes include not checking i > 0 first (leading to index errors), forgetting that '01' is 1 (invalid), or incorrectly using string comparison instead of numeric. Real code pattern: def isValidTwoDigit(i, s): return i > 0 and 10 <= int(s[i-1:i+1]) <= 26.

examples.trace: i=1 > 0 ✓; substring s[0:2]='27'; int('27')=27; 10 ≤ 27 ≤ 26 ✗; return False

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Checking Single-Digit Decoding Validity
ladders.desc: Determine if a single digit can be decoded as a letter (1-9 maps to A-I).
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation checks whether a single character (digit) can be decoded on its own. In the encoding scheme, digits 1 through 9 map to letters A through I. Digit '0' does not map to any letter when alone—it can only be part of a two-digit number like 10 or 20. Therefore, the condition is simply: s[i] != '0'. The intuition is that '0' represents no letter by itself in this mapping; it must be paired with a preceding '1' or '2' to form 10 or 20. Implementation: if s[i] != '0': valid single-digit decode. Time complexity is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). This feeds into L3 as a prerequisite for the full validation logic. Common mistakes include checking for '1' through '9' explicitly (which works but is verbose) or forgetting that '0' is invalid. Real code pattern: def isValidSingleDigit(i, s): return s[i] != '0'.

examples.trace: s[0] = '0'; '0' != '0' ✗; return False

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Digit to Letter Mapping
ladders.desc: Learn the basic encoding scheme where digits 1-26 map to letters A-Z.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding how the decoding works. Each letter from A to Z is encoded as a number from 1 to 26. Specifically: 1->A, 2->B, 3->C, ..., 9->I, 10->J, 11->K, ..., 26->Z. A string of digits can be decoded by splitting it into substrings that are valid numbers in this range. For example, '12' can be decoded as 'AB' (1 and 2) or 'L' (12). The intuition is that we need to count all possible ways to partition the digit string into valid encoding numbers. This is foundational because all higher levels build on this mapping. Time and space analysis are not applicable here as it's just a definition. This connects to L4 by explaining why single digits 1-9 are valid and why two-digit numbers 10-26 are valid. Common mistakes include thinking 0 maps to a letter (it doesn't), or that numbers above 26 can be decoded (they can't in this problem). Real code pattern: No code—just understanding the mapping: mapping = {1:'A', 2:'B', ..., 26:'Z'}.

examples.trace: 1 maps to A in the encoding scheme.
26 maps to Z in the encoding scheme.
